answers.q: Is there any evidence that God exists?
answers.meta: Yes - several independent lines of evidence point to God: the universe's beginning, its fine-tuning, morality, and human consciousness.
answers.a:
Yes - and you don't have to take it on blind faith. Several independent lines of evidence point toward God. The universe had a beginning, which calls for a cause beyond space and time. Its physics is finely tuned for life against staggering odds. We experience real moral obligations that are hard to ground in mere matter. And human reason, consciousness, and our longing for meaning fit a personal source better than a blind one.

None of these is a knockdown proof by itself, but together they make belief in God reasonable - arguably more reasonable than the alternatives. Faith here isn't a leap in the dark; it's trust built on cumulative evidence.

answers.q: What caused the universe?
answers.meta: Modern cosmology traces the universe to a finite beginning - and whatever begins to exist has a cause that is timeless, spaceless, and powerful.
answers.a:
Modern cosmology traces space, time, and matter back to what looks like a finite beginning; the most reasonable reading of the evidence is that the universe has not always existed. But whatever begins to exist has a cause; things don't simply pop into being from nothing. So the universe has a cause.

And the cause of all space, time, and matter cannot itself be spatial, temporal, or material. It must be timeless, spaceless, immaterial, enormously powerful, and - since it brought about a beginning - plausibly personal. That's a remarkable amount to learn from one question, and it lines up strikingly with what believers have always meant by God. "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1).

answers.q: Why is there something rather than nothing?
answers.meta: Everything in the universe could have failed to exist. That points to a necessary being that exists by its own nature - the reason anything exists.
answers.a:
Everything in the universe exists - but nothing about it had to. It could all have failed to exist; it is "contingent." A contingent thing needs an explanation outside itself for why it exists rather than not.

And you can't explain the whole collection of contingent things by appealing to still more contingent things - that only pushes the question back a step. The chain has to rest on something that exists by its own nature and cannot fail to exist: a necessary being. That isn't just one more item in the universe; it is the reason there is a universe at all. Christians recognise that being as the God who said, "I AM WHO I AM" (Exodus 3:14).

answers.q: Is the universe fine-tuned for life?
answers.meta: The constants of physics sit in an extraordinarily narrow life-permitting range. Design is a serious explanation alongside chance and necessity.
answers.a:
The fundamental constants of physics - the strength of gravity, the balance of forces, the expansion rate of the universe - sit within an extraordinarily narrow range that permits life. Tweak many of them by a tiny fraction and no stars, planets, or chemistry could form.

There are three options: this fine-tuning is due to physical necessity, to chance, or to design. Necessity has no evidence behind it; chance has to invoke an unobserved multiverse to explain away the odds. Design remains a serious, simpler explanation. As the psalmist saw long before the physics, "The heavens declare the glory of God" (Psalm 19:1).

answers.q: Where do morality and human rights come from without God?
answers.meta: If some things are truly wrong - not just disliked - that binding moral law points to a moral lawgiver beyond human opinion.
answers.a:
Most of us know that some things - torturing a child for fun, for example - are not merely distasteful but genuinely wrong. The question is what could make them really wrong.

The challenge is not that atheists can't be good - they clearly can be, and often are - but grounding: without a moral lawgiver it is very hard to say what makes cruelty objectively wrong rather than merely something we dislike. Some atheists reply that moral facts simply exist on their own, though it is far from clear what such facts are or why they would bind us. And we really do believe such acts would be wrong even if everyone approved of them. A moral law that binds everyone, everywhere, points beyond human preference to a moral lawgiver. Your deepest moral instinct is pointing at something - and Someone - real. "He has shown you... what is good" (Micah 6:8).

answers.q: Can you be good without believing in God?
answers.meta: Of course atheists can be good people. The real question isn't whether you can act morally without God, but what grounds morality at all.
answers.a:
Absolutely - atheists can be kind, honest, and self-sacrificing, often putting believers to shame. Christianity has never claimed otherwise; Scripture says God's moral law is "written on the heart" of everyone (Romans 2:15).

But that's not the real question. The question isn't whether you can recognise or follow morality without believing in God - it's what makes anything truly good or evil in the first place. You can enjoy the meal without knowing who cooked it. Likewise, people can live morally while denying the very foundation that makes morality more than personal taste. Goodness without God is possible to practise, but hard to ground.

answers.q: Doesn't science disprove God?
answers.meta: Science studies how the physical world works; it isn't equipped to rule out a Creator. Many founders of modern science were devout believers.
answers.a:
Science is the study of how the physical world works - its methods are designed to find natural mechanisms, not to detect or rule out a Creator who stands behind nature itself. Asking science to disprove God is like asking a metal detector to find justice; it's the wrong instrument for the question.

Far from being enemies, modern science grew up in a Christian worldview that expected an orderly, intelligible universe made by a rational God. Kepler, Newton, Faraday, Maxwell, and many founders of their fields were devout believers. Science explains the mechanism; it doesn't remove the need for a Mind behind the mechanism.

answers.q: Did Jesus really exist?
answers.meta: Virtually all historians - including non-Christians - agree Jesus of Nazareth was a real first-century figure who was crucified under Pilate.
answers.a:
Yes. The existence of Jesus of Nazareth is one of the most secure facts in ancient history, accepted by virtually all scholars - including sceptics and non-Christians. We have not only the New Testament documents (multiple early, independent sources) but also non-Christian references from Roman and Jewish writers such as Tacitus and Josephus.

Historians take it as established that Jesus lived in first-century Palestine, was known as a teacher and healer, gathered followers, and was crucified under Pontius Pilate. The interesting debates are not about whether he existed, but about who he was and what happened after his death.

answers.q: Is there evidence that Jesus rose from the dead?
answers.meta: A core set of facts most historians accept - the crucifixion, empty tomb, appearances, and the disciples' transformation - is best explained by the resurrection.
answers.a:
Yes - and the case rests on facts accepted by most historians, not just believers. Jesus was crucified and buried. His tomb was found empty (the most disputed item on this list). Many different people, in different settings, sincerely reported seeing him alive afterwards. And former sceptics like Paul and James were transformed into bold witnesses willing to die.

The debate among historians isn't usually about these facts; it's about what explains them. Naturalistic theories - hallucination, theft, legend - each struggle to account for all of them at once. The resurrection explains the whole set simply and directly. "He is not here; he has risen" (Matthew 28:6).

answers.q: Wasn't the resurrection just a legend that grew over time?
answers.meta: The resurrection claim traces to within a few years of the cross - far too early for legend. 1 Corinthians 15 preserves a creed scholars date to within ~5 years.
answers.a:
The legend theory needs time - generations for a story to grow and the eyewitnesses to die off. The evidence doesn't allow it. Tucked inside 1 Corinthians 15 is an early creed that Paul says he "received" and "passed on," listing the death, burial, resurrection, and appearances of Jesus. Even sceptical scholars date this creed to within about five years of the crucifixion.

That is not a legend with room to develop; it's a report tracing straight back to the people who were there - including, the creed says, more than five hundred witnesses, most still living and available to be questioned (1 Corinthians 15:6).

answers.q: Why does it matter that women were the first witnesses to the empty tomb?
answers.meta: In a culture that discounted female testimony, no one inventing a story would make women the key witnesses. It's a fingerprint of history, not fiction.
answers.a:
All four Gospels report that the first witnesses to the empty tomb were women. In first-century Jewish and Roman culture, a woman's testimony was so devalued it often wasn't even accepted in a court of law.

If you were fabricating a story to convince that society, you would never hand the most important moment to witnesses no one would believe. You'd put respected men there instead. The fact that the Gospel writers kept this embarrassing detail is a strong sign they were reporting what actually happened rather than crafting effective propaganda. Awkward, unhelpful details like this are a fingerprint of history.

answers.q: Could the disciples have stolen Jesus' body?
answers.meta: The theft theory was the earliest counter-claim - but it concedes the tomb was empty, and it can't explain why the disciples died for what they'd know was a lie.
answers.a:
This was the very first counter-explanation - Matthew records that the authorities claimed "his disciples came... and stole him away" (Matthew 28:13). Notice what it already concedes: the tomb really was empty.

But the theory collapses on motive. The disciples went on to suffer beatings, imprisonment, and execution for proclaiming the risen Jesus. People will die for something they sincerely believe is true. They do not die for something they know is a lie - and conspirators inventing a hoax would know. A stolen body explains the empty tomb but not the transformed, fearless, suffering witnesses who refused to recant.

answers.q: Did the disciples just hallucinate the risen Jesus?
answers.meta: Hallucinations are private and individual. The resurrection appearances were to groups, on different occasions, including skeptics - and the tomb was still empty.
answers.a:
Hallucinations are real, but they don't fit the evidence. By their nature they are private, individual experiences - like dreams, two people can't share the same one. Yet Jesus was reportedly seen by individuals and by groups, indoors and outdoors, on multiple occasions, even by sceptics and enemies like Paul.

Hallucinations also wouldn't produce an empty tomb; the body would still be there for anyone to produce. And they don't explain the conversion of hostile witnesses who had no expectation or desire to see Jesus alive. The appearances behave like encounters with something real and external, not like wishful projections of grief.

answers.q: Why did Paul go from persecuting Christians to dying for Jesus?
answers.meta: Paul was an enemy of the church with everything to lose. His sudden, costly reversal - he said he saw the risen Jesus - is hard to explain away.
answers.a:
Paul - then called Saul - was no neutral observer. He was actively hunting Christians, approving executions and dragging believers to prison. He had status, training, and conviction invested in stamping the movement out.

Then, by his own account in our earliest written sources, the risen Jesus appeared to him on the road to Damascus. He didn't gain power or comfort; he traded everything for beatings, prison, and eventual execution. People will die for a belief they received from others, but Paul was in a position to know what he had or hadn't seen. The persecutor became a martyr because something happened he could not deny. "Last of all he appeared to me also" (1 Corinthians 15:8).

answers.q: Has the Bible been changed over the centuries?
answers.meta: No core teaching rests on a disputed line. With 5,800+ Greek manuscripts, scholars can trace and correct copying differences - the text is well preserved.
answers.a:
It's a common worry - a "game of telephone" over two thousand years. But that's not how manuscript copying works, and the evidence shows the opposite. The New Testament survives in over 5,800 Greek manuscripts, plus many thousands more in other languages - by far the best-attested text of the ancient world.

That abundance is a strength: scholars can compare copies and map exactly where differences crept in. The vast majority are spelling and word-order variations, and not one core Christian doctrine hangs on a disputed line. The Dead Sea Scrolls confirmed the same for the Old Testament. The text wasn't changed; it was carefully preserved.
answers.relatedLabel: Biblical Reliability in the Evidence Library

answers.q: Can we trust the New Testament manuscripts?
answers.meta: The New Testament is the best-attested document of antiquity by a wide margin - 5,800+ Greek copies versus a handful for most ancient works.
answers.a:
By any standard historians apply to ancient writings, yes. Compare the numbers: Caesar's Gallic Wars survives in about 10 copies, the average classical work in a few dozen to a few hundred. The New Testament survives in more than 5,800 Greek manuscripts, some within a generation or two of the originals.

No one doubts what Caesar or Tacitus wrote, despite far thinner evidence. The New Testament gives us more manuscripts, earlier, with more agreement. The question was never whether the text was copied - of course it was - but whether it was copied reliably. By the historian's own measuring stick, the answer is yes.

answers.q: Aren't there contradictions in the Bible?
answers.meta: Most alleged contradictions are differences in perspective or emphasis - exactly what independent eyewitness accounts look like, not copying errors.
answers.a:
Most alleged contradictions turn out to be differences of perspective, emphasis, or detail - the kind you'd expect from independent witnesses describing the same event. One Gospel mentions one angel at the tomb, another mentions two; that's not a contradiction (two includes one), it's two writers highlighting different things.

Ironically, if the accounts matched word-for-word, sceptics would (rightly) suspect collusion. The variations actually point to genuine, independent testimony. Many puzzles dissolve with cultural and historical context. There are hard passages worth wrestling with honestly - but "the Bible is riddled with contradictions" doesn't survive a careful, case-by-case look.

answers.q: Were the Gospels written by eyewitnesses?
answers.meta: The Gospels are rooted in eyewitness testimony, written within living memory of the events, with incidental details that fit first-century Palestine.
answers.a:
The Gospels were written within the lifetime of people who knew Jesus - decades after the events, not centuries. They draw directly on eyewitness testimony: Matthew and John are tied to apostles, Mark to Peter's preaching, and Luke explicitly says he investigated "those who from the first were eyewitnesses" (Luke 1:2).

They also carry the marks of real memory - accurate, incidental details of first-century Palestinian geography, names, customs, and politics that a later, distant author would be unlikely to get right. They read like reports anchored in testimony, not like myths composed long afterward in a faraway place.

answers.q: Is there archaeological evidence for the Bible?
answers.meta: Hundreds of finds - the Pilate Stone, Caiaphas ossuary, Hezekiah's tunnel, and more - confirm people, places, and events the Bible describes.
answers.a:
Yes - repeatedly. Archaeology can't prove theological claims, but it can test whether the Bible gets its world right, and again and again it does. The Pilate Stone confirms the Roman governor who tried Jesus. An ossuary names the high priest Caiaphas. Hezekiah's tunnel and the Tel Dan inscription ("House of David") confirm Old Testament figures sceptics once doubted.

Figures like Pontius Pilate, the politarchs of Thessalonica, and many cities and customs in Luke-Acts have been verified by inscriptions and excavation. A document that proves accurate on the checkable details earns trust on the rest. The biblical writers were rooted in real history.
answers.relatedLabel: Biblical Reliability in the Evidence Library

answers.q: Did Jesus actually claim to be God?
answers.meta: Yes - in word and action. He took God's own name ("Before Abraham was, I AM"), forgave sins, accepted worship, and said "I and the Father are one."
answers.a:
"Jesus never claimed to be God" is a popular line, but the New Testament tells a different story - in both words and actions. He said, "Before Abraham was born, I AM" (John 8:58), taking God's own name from Exodus; his hearers picked up stones, knowing exactly what he meant. He forgave sins - something only God can do - and accepted worship rather than refusing it.

He said, "I and the Father are one," and "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father." When Thomas cried, "My Lord and my God!" Jesus didn't correct him. These are not the words of a mere teacher; they are a claim to be God in the flesh (John 1:1, 14). Some argue the loftiest sayings reflect John's own theology - but the claim is not confined to John: in our earliest Gospel, Jesus forgives sins on his own authority (Mark 2:5-7) and takes to himself the divine Son of Man of Daniel 7, so his divine self-understanding runs through the earliest layers, not only the latest.
answers.relatedLabel: Jesus as God in the New Testament

answers.q: Isn't the Trinity a contradiction?
answers.meta: No. The Trinity says God is one in being and three in persons - different categories. It would only contradict if it claimed one God and three Gods.
answers.a:
It would be a contradiction if Christians claimed God is one person and three persons, or one God and three Gods, in the same sense. But that's not the doctrine. The Trinity says God is one in being (one "what") and three in persons (three "whos") - Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Those are different categories, so there's no logical contradiction, any more than one triangle having three sides is a contradiction.

It is a mystery - something we couldn't have guessed and can't fully exhaust - but a mystery is not a contradiction. The doctrine arose because Scripture insists there is one God, yet calls Father, Son, and Spirit each fully God.
answers.relatedLabel: The Trinity in the New Testament

answers.q: Wasn't Jesus just a good moral teacher?
answers.meta: Given his claims to deity, "good teacher" isn't on the menu. As C.S. Lewis argued, a man saying such things is either Lord, liar, or lunatic.
answers.a:
It sounds respectful, but it's the one option Jesus didn't leave open. A good moral teacher doesn't claim to forgive sins, accept worship, and share God's own name - unless those claims are true.

C.S. Lewis put it bluntly: a man who said the sorts of things Jesus said would either be a liar, a lunatic, or exactly who he claimed to be. He could be the Lord, or a deceiver, or self-deluded - but not merely a wise human teacher, because wise human teachers don't make themselves the center of their own message. The one option Lewis left off is the modern one: that Jesus never said these things, and the claims were legends added later. But the divine self-claims sit in our earliest and best-attested sources, not only the latest, so putting the words in his mouth after the fact does not fit the evidence either. You're free to reject his claim, but "great moral teacher" isn't a way to dodge it.

answers.q: Did the church invent Jesus' divinity at the Council of Nicaea?
answers.meta: No. Christians worshipped Jesus as divine from the earliest decades. Nicaea (325) didn't invent his deity - it defended language to express it.
answers.a:
This is a popular myth (helped along by novels), but the history is clear. Belief in Jesus' divinity wasn't invented at Nicaea in AD 325 - it's there in the earliest layers of the New Testament, decades earlier. Paul, writing in the 50s, applies Old Testament "Lord" texts about God directly to Jesus, and an early hymn calls him "in very nature God" (Philippians 2:6).

Nicaea didn't vote Jesus into divinity; it gathered to defend the language that protected what Christians already believed and worshipped, against a teaching (Arianism) that denied it. The council clarified and confessed an ancient faith - it didn't manufacture a new one.
answers.relatedLabel: The Early Church and the Trinity

answers.q: Did Jesus fulfill Old Testament prophecy?
answers.meta: Isaiah 53, written centuries before Jesus and preserved in the Dead Sea Scrolls, describes a pierced, silent, buried-then-vindicated servant who suffers for others.
answers.a:
Centuries before Jesus, Isaiah 53 describes a servant who is despised, pierced for others' sins, silent before his accusers, assigned a grave with the wicked and the rich, and then vindicated and "sees the light of life." The Great Isaiah Scroll from Qumran, dated around 125 BC, proves this text predates Jesus by over a century - it could not have been written after the fact.

Jewish and Christian readers have long debated who the servant is, but the portrait fits Jesus with startling precision. It's one of many threads - from Micah's Bethlehem to Daniel's timeline - that the New Testament presents as converging on him. Read Isaiah 53 yourself and ask who it describes.

answers.q: If God is good, why is there so much suffering?
answers.meta: This is the hardest question in faith - and it cuts both ways: calling suffering truly evil assumes a real standard of good that points back to God.
answers.a:
This is the deepest and most painful objection, and it deserves honesty, not a tidy slogan. Innocent people suffer; prayers seem unanswered. That weight is real.

But notice something. The moment you call suffering not just unpleasant but genuinely evil - truly wrong - you've assumed a real standard of how things ought to be. A purely indifferent universe has no "ought"; it can host pain, but not evil. So the very force of the objection borrows from a moral order that fits God better than a godless world. Christianity doesn't offer a formula; it offers a God who entered the suffering himself, and who promises to "wipe every tear from their eyes" (Revelation 21:4).
answers.relatedLabel: The Problem of Evil as Evidence

answers.q: How can a loving God send people to hell?
answers.meta: Hell is best understood not as God forcing people away, but as God honoring the freedom of those who finally refuse him. Love that can't be refused isn't love.
answers.a:
It helps to reframe the picture. God doesn't gleefully dispatch people to hell; the New Testament shows a God who "wants all people to be saved" (1 Timothy 2:4) and who went to the cross to rescue them.

But genuine love requires the freedom to refuse it - a love you cannot say no to wouldn't be love at all. Hell, as writers like C.S. Lewis describe it, is what it means for God to finally honor the choice of someone who has spent their whole life saying, "Leave me alone." The door, Lewis said, is locked from the inside. God's justice and his respect for human freedom meet there - and the cross shows how far he goes to spare us it.

answers.q: Could evil actually be evidence FOR God?
answers.meta: Surprisingly, yes. To call something objectively evil presupposes a real standard of good - and that standard is hard to ground without God.
answers.a:
It sounds backwards, but follow the logic. To say something is objectively evil - not just "I dislike it" - you have to measure it against a real standard of good. Where does that standard come from?

In a purely material universe of atoms and forces, there is no way things "ought" to be; there are only events. So the atheist who presses the problem of evil is quietly standing on a moral reality that atheism can't easily supply. As C.S. Lewis recounted, his own argument against God from the world's injustice forced the question: where did he get the idea of "just" and "unjust"? Evil is a genuine weight on faith - but it's an even harder problem for a world without God.
answers.relatedLabel: The Problem of Evil as Evidence

answers.q: Where is God when I'm hurting?
answers.meta: Christianity's answer isn't a formula but a Person - a God who entered suffering himself in Jesus and promises to be near the brokenhearted.
answers.a:
This is less an argument to win than a wound to sit with, so let me be gentle. Christianity's distinct answer to pain isn't a clever explanation - it's a God who didn't stay at a safe distance from it. In Jesus, God entered the world of grief, betrayal, torture, and death himself. Whatever you're facing, you're not facing it watched by a stranger.

Scripture says "the Lord is close to the brokenhearted" (Psalm 34:18), and that nothing - not trouble or anguish - can separate you from his love (Romans 8:38-39). Doubt and lament are not faithlessness; the Bible is full of both. If you're in a dark place right now, that's exactly where this God has promised to meet people.

answers.q: Aren't all religions basically the same?
answers.meta: They share some ethics, but make contradictory claims about God, humanity, and salvation. They can't all be true - though they can all be examined.
answers.a:
They share some moral overlap - most teach compassion and honesty - but on the biggest questions they flatly disagree. Is God personal or impersonal? One or many? Is the problem ignorance, imbalance, or sin? Is salvation earned, escaped, or received as a gift? These are contradictory answers, not different paths up one mountain.

Saying "they're all the same" actually disrespects each faith by ignoring what it really claims. They can't all be true - but they can all be examined. Christianity is unusual in resting on a historical claim you can investigate: that a real man, in real history, died and rose again. That's testable in a way "all paths lead to God" is not.

answers.q: Isn't faith just believing without evidence?
answers.meta: Biblical faith isn't blind - it means trust based on good reasons. The New Testament repeatedly appeals to evidence, witnesses, and reasoning.
answers.a:
That's a modern redefinition, not the biblical one. In Scripture, faith means trust - the kind you place in a person you have good reason to rely on, like trusting a surgeon or a friend. It's not pretending to know what you don't.

The New Testament constantly appeals to evidence: eyewitnesses, fulfilled prophecy, public miracles, and reasoned argument. Luke writes so his reader may have "certainty" (Luke 1:4); Paul reasons in the synagogues; Jesus points to what people have seen. Christianity invites investigation - "come and see." Faith and evidence aren't opposites; biblical faith is trust that rests on good reasons, then commits beyond what can be proven with mathematical certainty - which is how we live most of life anyway.

answers.q: Is it okay to have doubts as a Christian?
answers.meta: Yes. Doubt isn't the opposite of faith - it's often faith seeking understanding. Scripture is full of honest questions, and the answer is to investigate, not suppress.
answers.a:
Yes - and you're in good company. The Bible is full of honest doubt: the psalmists cry "How long, O Lord?", John the Baptist sends to ask if Jesus is really the one, and Thomas refuses to believe until he sees. Jesus doesn't crush any of them; he meets them.

Doubt isn't the opposite of faith - apathy is. Often doubt is faith seeking understanding. The healthy response isn't to suppress the questions or to abandon ship, but to bring them into the light and investigate. That's the whole reason a place like this exists: there are real, satisfying answers to most of the hard questions, and a faith that has examined itself stands far stronger than one that never asked.

answers.q: Has the Quran been changed or corrupted?
answers.meta: Muslims are taught the Quran is perfectly preserved, but its textual history — competing canonical readings and the Uthmanic standardisation — tells a more complicated story.
answers.a:
Muslims are often taught that the Quran has been preserved letter-for-letter, unchanged since Muhammad. The textual history is more complicated. There is not one single Quran text in use today but several canonical 'readings' (qira'at) — most of the Muslim world uses the Hafs reading, while much of North and West Africa uses Warsh, and the two differ in wording in places. Islam's own most trusted source (Sahih al-Bukhari 4987) reports that within a generation of Muhammad's death the caliph Uthman standardised one version and had the others burned — which is hard to square with a text that was already perfectly and identically preserved everywhere.

This is not said to mock a book Muslims love, but to invite an honest question: the strong claim of flawless, letter-perfect preservation only needs a few solid counter-examples to fall, and the qira'at and the Uthmanic standardisation are exactly that. A thoughtful Muslim will respond that these readings descend from the divinely permitted ahruf, so they are revelation faithfully transmitted, not corruption - but that reframing concedes the decisive point: there is no single letter-perfect text, and one caliph burned the variants other Muslims were using. The deeper point for a Christian conversation is that the case for Christianity never rested on a claim of mechanical preservation in the first place — it rests on the historical evidence for the resurrection.

answers.q: Was Jesus a Muslim?
answers.meta: Muslims say Jesus was a Muslim — a submitter to God. He did submit to the Father, but he also claimed and accepted what no mere submitter could: to forgive sins and receive worship.
answers.a:
Muslims often say Jesus (Isa) was a Muslim, meaning a 'submitter' to the one God — and in that broad sense, yes, Jesus submitted to his Father perfectly. But 'submitter to God' is not the same as a follower of the religion of Islam, which is defined by the testimony that Muhammad is God's messenger and by the Quran — both from the seventh century, six hundred years after Jesus. Jesus was a first-century Jew who never heard of Muhammad and kept the Torah, not Islamic law.

More decisively, the Jesus of the historical record did things no Muslim ever could: he forgave sins in his own name (Mark 2:5-7), accepted worship (John 20:28-29), and claimed to share the Father's glory (John 17:5) — which on Islam's own terms would be shirk, impossible for a prophet. A Muslim may reply that the Gospels were corrupted at exactly these points (tahrif) - but the divine-claim material sits in the earliest and best-attested strata, so excising only the inconvenient parts is special pleading. So either we trust the Gospels, and Jesus was no Muslim, or we discard them, and there is no historical 'Muslim Jesus' left at all. His submission to the Father was real — the submission of God the Son, who became human to save us (Philippians 2:6-8).
answers.relatedLabel: Was Jesus a Muslim? The Christian Response

answers.q: Does the Bible predict Muhammad?
answers.meta: Islam claims the Bible foretold Muhammad (the 'Paraclete' of John, the prophet of Deuteronomy 18). Read in context, these passages point elsewhere.
answers.a:
Islam teaches that Muhammad was foretold in the Bible, most often pointing to the 'Paraclete' Jesus promised in John 14-16 and the 'prophet like Moses' of Deuteronomy 18:15-18. Read in their own context, neither fits. Jesus identifies the Paraclete explicitly as the Holy Spirit who would come upon the disciples and dwell in them (John 14:17, 26) — not a human prophet arriving centuries later in Arabia. The 'prophet like Moses' is drawn from among the Israelites ('from among your brothers'), and the New Testament applies it to Jesus (Acts 3:22).

The claim usually depends on substituting a similar-sounding Greek word (periklytos, 'praised one') for parakletos ('helper'), but no Greek manuscript supports that reading. None of this is meant to disparage Muslims' sincerity — it is simply that the texts, read honestly in context, do not point to Muhammad.

answers.q: If God made everything, who made God?
answers.meta: If God made everything, who made God? The question misreads the claim: whatever begins to exist has a cause, and God did not begin to exist.
answers.a:
It is a fair and natural question, and it feels like it should stop the whole conversation. If everything needs a maker, then surely God needs one too, and the whole idea collapses into an infinite regress. Many thoughtful people have set the matter down right here.

But it rests on a small misunderstanding of what is actually being claimed. The argument for God from the universe's beginning never says everything has a cause. It says something narrower and more careful: whatever begins to exist has a cause. Things don't simply pop into being out of nothing; they come from something. The universe, the evidence suggests, began to exist, so it needs a cause beyond itself. God, by definition, did not begin to exist. He is eternal, without beginning. So he is not an arbitrary exception to the rule; he simply falls outside a rule that was only ever about things that begin.

Asking what made God is a bit like asking what the bachelor's wife is doing tonight. The question presupposes something the case has already ruled out. You are welcome to ask instead whether an uncaused, eternal being is coherent, or whether the universe might itself be eternal, and those are good questions worth pursuing. But once the claim is stated precisely, who made God stops being the knockdown it first appears to be. Whatever is the ultimate explanation of everything must, by its nature, be something that did not need to be made.
answers.relatedLabel: Did the Universe Begin? The Kalam Cosmological Argument

answers.q: Was Jesus copied from pagan myths like Horus and Mithras?
answers.meta: Was the resurrection borrowed from pagan dying-and-rising gods like Osiris or Mithras? The parallels are real but loose, late, and don't fit.
answers.a:
This is worth taking seriously, not waving away. There really are ancient stories of gods who descend into death and return, celebrated in the mystery cults of Osiris, Adonis, and others, and Christianity did spread in exactly that religious world. To pretend none of that exists would be intellectually cheap. At the surface, the resemblance is real enough to deserve an answer.

But when they are examined closely, they do not hold up as the source of Easter, and the strongest critics here are not apologists but historians of religion. Jonathan Z. Smith, the skeptical University of Chicago scholar, concluded that the whole dying-and-rising category is largely a misnomer built on late and ambiguous texts. Where genuine cases exist, they are gods bound to the seasonal cycle, dying and reviving with the crops each year, in a timeless mythic elsewhere. The Christian claim is a different kind of thing: a named man, under a named governor, Pontius Pilate, in a known city, claimed to have risen once, bodily, never to die again. Figures like Mithras and the Egyptian Horus don't actually match the story at all once you read the sources rather than the memes.

And the positive case doesn't rest on the Gospels being ancient and unique alone. It rests on how early the belief is. Paul quotes a creed in 1 Corinthians 15 that scholars date to within a few years of the crucifixion, far too early for legend to have slowly grown. The right verdict is not that the resurrection is proven, but that it was something new, which its environment cannot explain away.
answers.relatedLabel: Was the Resurrection Borrowed from Pagan Myths?

answers.q: Who decided which books are in the Bible?
answers.meta: Who decided the books of the Bible? Not Constantine or Nicaea. The core was received early and recognized over centuries by principled criteria.
answers.a:
The popular version of this story is that a council, often the Council of Nicaea in 325 under the Emperor Constantine, voted on which books to include and suppressed the rest. It makes for a gripping novel, but almost every detail is false. Nicaea did not discuss, vote on, or even record raising the question of the canon; its business was the relationship of Christ to the Father. Historians across the spectrum, including the agnostic scholar Bart Ehrman, reject the Constantine myth outright.

What actually happened was slower and more careful. The canon was not decided in a single moment; it was recognized over centuries. Paul's letters were being collected in the first century. By around 180 the four Gospels were a fixed fact, and lists like the Muratorian Fragment already look substantially like our New Testament. Before Nicaea ever met, Eusebius shows a careful sorting of books already underway, using real criteria: apostolic origin, agreement with the church's inherited faith, and widespread use across the scattered churches. The disputed books were genuinely disputed for generations, which is evidence of care, not conspiracy. When councils like Hippo and Carthage listed the books at the end of the fourth century, they were ratifying a consensus long since reached, not voting books into being.

One honest caveat: Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants still differ over a handful of Old Testament books, and both sides can point to ancient precedent. That is a real, reasonable disagreement this answer won't try to settle. But it touches only the margins, and it leaves the whole invented-by-an-emperor story untouched.
answers.relatedLabel: How Was the Bible's Canon Decided?

answers.q: Do Jehovah's Witnesses believe Jesus is God?
answers.meta: Do Jehovah's Witnesses believe Jesus is God? No. They hold he is God's first and greatest creature. Here is that view answered with respect.
answers.a:
No, and it helps to state their view accurately rather than caricature it. Jehovah's Witnesses honor Jesus highly, but they hold that only the Father, whom they call Jehovah, is God in the full sense. On their teaching, the Son is God's first and greatest creation, the one being Jehovah made directly, who then served as the agent through whom everything else was made. Before his earthly life, they teach, he was the archangel Michael. This is their case at its strongest, and a sincere Witness can defend it from Scripture with real skill, leaning especially on texts like Colossians calling Christ firstborn of creation and John 14:28, the Father is greater than I.

The historic Christian answer is that this is essentially the ancient view of Arius, which the Council of Nicaea weighed and rejected in 325, confessing instead that the Son is of the same nature as the Father, coequal and coeternal. The reply to firstborn is that the Greek word means rank and inheritance, not sequence; Scripture even calls David firstborn though he was Jesse's youngest. And Paul settles it in the very next line: for by him all things were created. A being for whom all things were made stands on the Creator's side, not among the things created.

The question between us, then, is not whether Jesus matters, but something sharp and clear: is Jesus a creature or the Creator? Christians believe John's own words answer it, the Word was God, and that this is the heart of the good news rather than a technicality to argue over coldly.
answers.relatedLabel: Answering Jehovah's Witnesses on the Trinity

answers.q: Is the Mormon Jesus the same as the Jesus of the Bible?
answers.meta: Is the Mormon Jesus the same as the Bible's Jesus? The names match, but the underlying doctrine of God differs in a real and significant way.
answers.a:
It is a sincere and important question, and it deserves a careful, respectful answer rather than a slogan. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints loves Jesus and centers its worship on him, and the sincerity of its members and their high regard for the worth of the human person should be honored, not mocked. But when we ask about the underlying doctrine of God, a real difference appears. In its own words, the LDS Church teaches that the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are three separate beings united in purpose, with the Father and the Son having tangible bodies of flesh and bone. Its most distinctive teaching, summarized in Lorenzo Snow's couplet, is that God the Father was once a man who progressed to godhood.

The historic Christian faith, shared by Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants, confesses something different: one God, numerically one divine being, who exists eternally as three coequal, coeternal persons. He did not become God, and there was never a God before him. Isaiah's monotheism is sweeping here: before me no god was formed, nor shall there be any after me. So the disagreement is narrow and sharp: is God one being or several, and did God become God, or is he eternally and unchangeably God?

The honest conclusion is that the two share a name and a deep love for Christ, but describe him within genuinely different accounts of who God is. That is not a reason for contempt; it is a reason for the kind of clear, gentle conversation that takes the difference seriously precisely because the answer matters so much.

answers.q: If Jesus is God, why did he pray to God and say he didn't know the day or hour?
answers.meta: If Jesus is God, why did he pray and say he didn't know the day or hour? Because in the incarnation the Son took on a real human nature.
answers.a:
This is one of the most honest and forceful objections there is, and it should be felt at full strength. Jesus prays. He obeys. He is sent. He says the Father is greater than I, and he confesses he does not know the day or the hour. How can someone who ranks himself below God, and is ignorant of what God knows, actually be God? Taken together these texts seem to hand us a subordinate being.

The classical Christian answer turns on the incarnation. Philippians 2 says the Son, being in the form of God, emptied himself by taking the form of a servant. Notice what the Greek does not say: it never says he emptied himself of his deity. There is no of anything in the sentence. It tells us how he emptied himself, by adding a genuine human nature, not by subtracting his divinity. The mystery is concealment, not loss: the one who was fully God veiled that glory beneath a servant's form, remaining what he was while becoming what he was not. So the praying, obeying, and not-knowing belong to his real human life and his freely accepted role as servant, not to an inferiority in the divine nature. This is not the ancient Arian idea that the Son is a lesser god.

And the same Jesus who says the Father is greater also says I and the Father are one. Greater speaks of order and role, not of a better nature. The picture that holds all the texts together is not a demoted deity, but God himself stooping to share our life, our limits, and even our death.
answers.relatedLabel: Philippians 2:6-11: The Carmen Christi and Christ's Deity

answers.q: Where is the Trinity in the Bible?
answers.meta: Where is the Trinity in the Bible? The word is later, but the data isn't: one God, with Father, Son, and Spirit each God and genuinely distinct.
answers.a:
It is a fair challenge, and the honest starting point is a concession: the word Trinity does not appear in the Bible. It was coined later to name a pattern the church found already there. But the absence of a label is not the absence of the thing. Words like monotheism, omnipotence, and even Bible aren't in Scripture either; summary terms exist to gather up what a whole body of texts teaches.

So look at the data the word gathers. First, the New Testament is fiercely monotheistic: there is one God, full stop. Second, that one God is spoken of as Father, as Son, and as Holy Spirit, and each is treated as fully God, the Word was God in John 1, the Spirit called Lord and giver of life. Third, the three are genuinely distinct: the Son prays to the Father in Gethsemane, is sent by the Father, and promises to send another Advocate, the Spirit. One who prays to another, is sent by another, and sends another is not a single person wearing three masks. And texts like the baptism of Jesus and the closing command of Matthew 28 to baptize in the one name of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit hold all three together.

The doctrine is simply the attempt to say all of that at once without dropping any of it: one God, eternally existing as three coequal, coeternal, genuinely distinct persons. Not three gods, which would be tritheism, and not one person in three costumes, which would be modalism. Deny the word if you like; every one of the biblical givens it names remains on the page.
answers.relatedLabel: The Trinity in the New Testament

answers.q: What is the minimal facts argument for the resurrection?
answers.meta: What is the minimal facts argument? It builds the case for the resurrection only on facts even skeptical scholars grant, then weighs explanations.
answers.a:
Most arguments for the resurrection stall on the first move: the case quotes the Gospels, and the skeptic replies that the Gospels are exactly what's in dispute. The minimal facts approach, developed over four decades by Gary Habermas and his student Michael Licona, is built to sidestep that stalemate. Instead of asking you to grant the reliability of the New Testament, it asks only for a short list of facts that meet two demanding tests: each is strongly evidenced by multiple lines of testimony, and each is accepted by the great majority of scholars who study the subject, including skeptical, agnostic, and Jewish ones.

The usual list runs like this: Jesus died by crucifixion; his disciples sincerely believed he rose and appeared to them; the persecutor Paul was converted by what he took to be an appearance of the risen Jesus; the skeptic James, Jesus's own brother, was likewise converted; and, flagged as more contested, the tomb was found empty. Even a hostile scholar like John Dominic Crossan grants that the crucifixion is as sure as anything historical can be. Then the argument asks, plainly: which single explanation best accounts for all of these at once?

The claim is not a mathematical proof but an inference to the best explanation, the reasoning a historian or detective uses. Each naturalistic theory, hallucination, theft, legend, can cover one fact or two but strains on the rest, while the resurrection covers them together. Honesty requires two caveats: the empty tomb is the softer, more disputed link, and the argument is strongest leaning on the facts critics themselves concede. It doesn't force the conclusion, but it puts it seriously on the table.

answers.q: Why did the disciples die for their belief in the resurrection?
answers.meta: Why did the disciples die for the resurrection? Their willingness to suffer shows sincerity, not that they were lying. But what does that prove?
answers.a:
First, a caution the honest version of this argument insists on: dying for a belief does not make it true. Muslims, Hindus, and countless others have died bravely for convictions that cannot all be correct. Sincerity is not proof. And the popular claim that all twelve apostles were martyred outruns the evidence; as the Christian apologist Sean McDowell has shown, only a few apostolic deaths, like Peter, Paul, and the two Jameses, are firmly attested. So the argument has to be stated carefully, not crudely.

What martyrdom does establish is sincerity, and that matters more than it first seems, because of who these particular people were. A modern martyr dies for a conviction received on trust from others. The original disciples claimed to be eyewitnesses of the very thing in question: that they had seen Jesus alive again. People will die for what they sincerely believe, but they do not, as a rule, knowingly die for what they themselves have fabricated, with nothing to gain. So their willingness to suffer and, for some, to die, closes off the crudest theory, that the whole thing was a deliberate hoax, and confirms that the belief we are trying to explain was genuinely and deeply held.

That leaves the real question open, which is where the interesting conversation begins: what best explains why these first-century Jews became utterly convinced they had seen a crucified man risen bodily from death, a category their own tradition did not even have? Their courage doesn't prove they were right. It proves they weren't lying, and that whatever happened, something happened.
answers.relatedLabel: Why Did the Disciples Believe Jesus Rose?

answers.q: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God?
answers.meta: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God? There is real overlap, but the God Christians worship is triune - and a picture of God that denies the Son does not reach him. A careful answer.
answers.a:
This is a question people feel strongly about - and one on which faithful, orthodox Christians genuinely disagree - so it deserves precision rather than a quick yes or no. There is a real overlap: both are monotheists who trace their worship back to the God of Abraham, the maker of heaven and earth, and both are fiercely committed to his oneness. A Muslim who recoils from the Trinity is guarding God's oneness, tawhid, and dreads the sin of shirk, associating a partner with God - an instinct against idolatry Christians can respect, since they too worship one God and reject every rival deity. But whether the two worship 'the same God' cannot be settled with a simple yes: the God Christians worship is not a solitary monad but the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, and Scripture ties having the Father to having the Son - 'No one who denies the Son has the Father' (1 John 2:23).

Where the two genuinely differ is in what they believe that one God is like in his own inner life. Christians do not believe in three gods; the church has condemned that as heresy for nearly two thousand years. Nor, despite one Qur'anic passage naming God, Jesus, and Mary, has any Christian creed ever placed Mary in the Godhead, and Christians reject that idea as firmly as any Muslim would. The Trinity is not a partner added to God from outside; that would indeed be shirk. It is a claim about what the one God eternally is within himself: an eternal communion of Father, Son, and Spirit in a single, undivided essence. Even the word son is meant relationally, not biologically, Arabic Christians say ibn Allah, never walad Allah, which would imply a physical child that Christians deny as strongly as the Qur'an does.

So the most honest answer is neither a confident 'yes, the same God' nor a dismissive 'no, a different God entirely.' There is genuine overlap in the One both intend - the Creator, the God of Abraham - but the God Christians actually worship is triune, and a picture of God that rejects the Son does not reach him as he has made himself known. That is not a difference to smooth over but to explore honestly and gently, as a conversation between people who both take God's oneness with utter seriousness - and it is why Christians long for their Muslim neighbours to come to know the Father through the Son.
answers.relatedLabel: Answering Islamic Objections to the Trinity

answers.q: Why were some gospels left out of the Bible?
answers.meta: Gospels like Thomas, Peter, and Judas were left out mainly for chronology, not a power play: they were written generations too late to be apostolic.
answers.a:
It is a fair question, and the popular answer - that a Roman emperor or a church council simply voted the winners in and suppressed the rest - deserves a fair hearing before it is set aside. The early Jesus movement really was more diverse than a tidy picture suggests, there really were competing texts, and the group that became orthodox really did prevail. To pretend otherwise would be to ignore the evidence. So the question deserves to be taken seriously, not waved away.

But the decisive reason those other gospels were left out was not theology or politics; it was chronology. The four canonical Gospels date to the first century, within living memory of the events. The Gospel of Thomas, the Gospel of Peter, and the Gospel of Judas are second-century or later compositions - the Gospel of Judas, recovered and published in 2006, is generally dated to around the mid-second century, far too late to come from Judas. They borrow the names of apostles who had long since died. The test the early church applied was historical: is this writing genuinely close to the apostles, and has it been read across the churches? The four Gospels passed; the later texts did not.

Worth saying plainly: recognizing which books are early and apostolic is not the same as proving their contents true - that is a further argument, on further grounds. And these Gnostic texts are not worthless curiosities; they tell us much about the movements that produced them. But they are not first-century apostolic testimony, and that, not a suppression campaign, is why they sit outside the canon.
answers.relatedLabel: How Was the Bible's Canon Decided?

answers.q: Is Jesus the same person as Michael the Archangel?
answers.meta: Jehovah's Witnesses identify Jesus with Michael the archangel. But Jude 9 and Hebrews 1 place the Son categorically above the angels.
answers.a:
Jehovah's Witnesses sincerely hold that before his earthly life the Son was the archangel Michael, and they build the case from Scripture: the Lord descends "with an archangel's voice" (1 Thessalonians 4:16), and Michael leads heaven's armies in Revelation 12 much as the returning Christ does. Together, that is their case at its strongest: a mighty Son who is, on their reading, nonetheless a creature. It is worth engaging on the texts, not a caricature of it.

The difficulty is that the inference is weaker than it looks, and Scripture itself draws a line it cannot cross. Descending "with an archangel's voice" no more makes Jesus an archangel than descending "with the trumpet of God" in the same verse makes him a trumpet - the word signals accompaniment, not identity. More decisively, in Jude 9 the archangel Michael, disputing with the devil, "did not presume to pronounce a blasphemous judgment, but said, 'The Lord rebuke you.'" Michael cannot rebuke Satan on his own authority; he must appeal to the Lord. Jesus rebukes Satan directly, on his own word.

And the whole first chapter of Hebrews is a sustained argument that the Son is categorically higher than the angels: "To which of the angels did God ever say, 'You are my Son'?" (Hebrews 1:5), and "Let all God's angels worship him" (1:6). A chapter written to prove the Son is not an angel is hard to reconcile with the claim that he is the chief one. Faithful angels refuse worship (Revelation 22:8-9); the risen Jesus receives it - "My Lord and my God!" (John 20:28) - and blesses it. The eternal Son is the Creator worshipped by the angels, not the greatest among them.
answers.relatedLabel: Answering Jehovah's Witnesses on the Trinity

answers.q: Are Mormons Christians?
answers.meta: It depends what you mean. Latter-day Saints love Jesus and self-identify as Christian, but their doctrine of God differs from the historic Nicene creed.
answers.a:
This is one of those questions where the honest answer is: it depends what you mean, and the distinction matters more than a yes or no. If "Christian" means someone who reveres Jesus Christ, seeks to follow him, and centers their life on him, then Latter-day Saints plainly qualify - many are devout, Christ-focused people, and it would be ungracious to pretend otherwise. That sincerity deserves respect, not a sneer.

But if "Christian" means holding the historic doctrine of God confessed in the Nicene Creed - the creed shared by Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants alike - then there is a real, and openly acknowledged, difference. Latter-day Saints themselves distinguish their teaching from the creeds. Their official Gospel Topics teaching states that the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are "three separate beings united in purpose," with the Father and Son having "tangible bodies of flesh and bones." Historic Christianity confesses one God, numerically one, in three coequal and coeternal persons, who did not become God and before whom there was no God. Those two pictures of God cannot both be right.

So the fair thing is to judge the doctrine, not the hearts. On the historic definition of the term, the LDS doctrine of God differs from Christian orthodoxy - and this is a difference the Church itself affirms. That is a genuine theological disagreement worth exploring with respect, not a verdict on anyone's sincerity or eternal standing, which is God's to render, not ours.

answers.q: Are Jehovah's Witnesses Christians?
answers.meta: It depends what you mean. Witnesses love Jesus and follow the Bible closely, but they deny his full deity and the Trinity confessed by the historic creeds.
answers.a:
As with similar questions, the honest answer turns on what "Christian" means, and the distinction is worth making carefully rather than crudely. If it means someone who honors Jesus, studies the Bible diligently, and tries to obey God, then Jehovah's Witnesses plainly qualify. The person at your door is, in most cases, sincere, often strikingly well-versed in Scripture, and genuinely trying to honor the oneness of God. That is not nothing, and it deserves respect.

But if "Christian" means holding the faith confessed in the Nicene Creed - the creed shared by Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants - then there is a real difference, and Witnesses are the first to say so. The Watchtower teaches that only the Father is God in the full sense; the Son is God's first and greatest creation, and the Holy Spirit is an impersonal "active force," not a person. Historic Christianity confesses that the Son is of the same nature as the Father - coequal, coeternal - one God in three persons. The question is precise: is Jesus a creature or the Creator? The historic church answers Creator, and points to texts like John 20:28, where Thomas calls the risen Jesus "My Lord and my God" and is blessed rather than corrected.

So the fair statement is that Witnesses self-identify as Christian and love Jesus - grant that warmly - but by the historic creedal definition their doctrine of God differs at its center. That is a disagreement about who Jesus is, to be pursued gently, one text at a time - not a judgment on anyone's heart, which belongs to God alone.
answers.relatedLabel: Answering Jehovah's Witnesses on the Trinity

answers.q: Does the Big Bang point to God?
answers.meta: The Big Bang is strong evidence the universe began - but a beginning is not by itself a proof of God. It is one step; the rest takes further reasoning.
answers.a:
It is honest to answer this carefully, because the argument is easy to overstate. The Big Bang does not, by itself, prove that God exists, and any case that claims it does has reached too far. What the evidence supports - a convergence of independent lines never designed to test anyone's theology - is the narrower and still remarkable conclusion that the universe had a beginning; it has not simply existed forever.

From there, a further step of reasoning is available. Whatever begins to exist has a cause - things do not pop into being from nothing. So if the universe began, it has a cause. And the cause of all space, time, and matter cannot itself be spatial, temporal, or material; it would have to be timeless, immaterial, and enormously powerful. That is a striking amount to infer, and it converges with what believers have long meant by God. But notice the word "whatever begins to exist" - the argument does not claim everything has a cause, only what comes into being.

The skeptic's caution deserves respect: the earliest instant lies in a regime where our physics may break down, and even the theorem often cited for a cosmic beginning is a classical result whose co-author, Alan Guth, remains open to a universe with no beginning at all. So a beginning is best held as the more reasonable inference on present evidence, not a demonstration. The Big Bang points in God's direction and clears away the idea of an eternal, self-sufficient cosmos - but getting all the way to the Father Almighty takes more than physics can carry on its own.
answers.relatedLabel: Does the Big Bang Point to God?

answers.q: Did Jesus die on the cross, or does the Quran deny it?
answers.meta: On its dominant reading the Quran denies Jesus was crucified. Yet his death by crucifixion is the best-attested fact about his life in history.
answers.a:
Here the two faiths genuinely diverge, and the Muslim position deserves to be stated fairly rather than brushed aside. The key text is Surah 4:157, which on its dominant reading denies that Jesus was crucified - "they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him, but it was made to appear so to them." The classical Islamic tradition often reads this as a substitution: God rescued his prophet and another was crucified in his place. It is worth adding that some Muslim scholars read the verse more subtly - as denying that Jesus' enemies truly triumphed over him, rather than denying his death outright - and a Christian who insists the Quran must mean the crude substitution story is not reading it as carefully as its best interpreters do.

That said, on the historical question the evidence is unusually one-sided. Jesus' death by crucifixion under Pontius Pilate is regarded by historians across the spectrum as among the most certain facts we have about him. The agnostic scholar Bart Ehrman calls it as sure as anything historical can be, and it is attested not only in the Gospels but in non-Christian sources like the Roman historian Tacitus. Crucifixion was also the last thing early followers would invent about a Messiah - a crucified Messiah was a scandal, not a selling point.

So the tension is real and should be named without triumphalism. The Quran, on its usual reading, sets aside an event that the historical record attests as firmly as almost anything from antiquity. That does not settle every question of faith, but on this particular point the historian and the Gospel writers stand together: Jesus really did die on a Roman cross.

answers.q: Is Jesus really 'a god' in John 1:1 (New World Translation)?
answers.meta: The New World Translation renders John 1:1 'the Word was a god.' Greek grammar points instead to a qualitative sense: the Word fully shares God's nature.
answers.a:
The Jehovah's Witnesses' rendering is not arbitrary, and it starts from a real feature of the Greek. In John 1:1's third clause, the Greek word for "God" (theos) lacks the definite article, whereas the second clause - "the Word was with God" - has it. Greek can use the article to mark definiteness, so the Witness argues that the article-less theos should read "a god," preserving the distinction between the Father and the Word. And they are right about one thing: the missing article is a real feature doing real work. John has just said the Word was "with" God; he is not about to say the Word simply is the Father. That error even has an ancient name - modalism - and historic orthodoxy rejects it too.

The problem is the leap from "no article" to "therefore 'a god.'" That inference does not hold in Greek. Philip Harner's 1973 study showed that anarthrous predicate nouns placed before the verb - exactly the construction here - are usually qualitative: they describe the nature of the subject rather than counting it. The clause means the Word had the very nature of God. Daniel Wallace's standard grammar reaches the same verdict: theos here is neither definite nor indefinite but qualitative - the Word fully shares the divine nature while remaining personally distinct from the Father.

The elegance is in the balance. Had John put the article on theos, he would have made the Word identical to the Father - modalism. Had he meant "a god," he would have made the Word a lesser divine being alongside God - polytheism, from an author steeped in Jewish monotheism. What he actually wrote threads the needle: the Word is fully God in nature, yet not the person of the Father. "A god" is the one reading the Greek and the context will not bear.
answers.relatedLabel: John 1:1 Explained: Is the Word God?

answers.q: Can science explain the origin of life without God?
answers.meta: There is currently no consensus scientific mechanism for life's origin. A design inference is one contested option - not proof, and not a rejection of evolution.
answers.a:
Fairness to the science comes first, because waving away decades of careful work is neither honest nor persuasive. Real progress has been made on sub-problems. The famous Miller-Urey experiment showed that some of life's building blocks, like amino acids, can form spontaneously - a genuine landmark. But it produced building blocks, not living systems, and certainly not the thing that has come to dominate the field: the origin of biological information, the coded instructions in the first self-replicating cell. And its assumed early atmosphere is now widely thought not to match the real early Earth. The honest summary is that there is at present no agreed scientific mechanism for the origin of life; researchers themselves describe the problem as open.

That opening is where a design inference enters - but it must be stated with care. The strongest version is not "science can't explain it, therefore God," which would be an argument from ignorance. It is an inference to the best explanation: in every other case we know, coded information traces back to a mind, so a mind is a serious candidate for the information in the first cell. This inference is contested and defeasible, not a proof. Absence of a current natural explanation is not proof that none exists; anyone who says abiogenesis is impossible has overreached.

One thing worth stressing: this question is not about evolution. The origin of life (abiogenesis) and the origin of species (evolution) are distinct problems - Darwin himself set the first aside. The design inference here concerns only the first cell, and it does not require rejecting common descent or evolutionary biology, on which sincere Christians hold a range of views. So science has not closed this question, and a mind remains a reasonable candidate among rivals - offered as an honest inference, not a knockdown proof.
answers.relatedLabel: The Origin of Life: Evidence and Design

answers.q: Does the multiverse explain away the fine-tuning of the universe?
answers.meta: The multiverse is a serious rival to design, not a scientific refutation of fine-tuning - and it seems to need fine-tuning of its own to get going.
answers.a:
The multiverse deserves respect, not dismissal, because it is a genuinely serious response. The fine-tuning data itself is not really in dispute: the constants of physics fall within strikingly narrow life-permitting ranges, a point conceded even by atheist physicists like Roger Penrose. The multiverse offers an elegant way to absorb that. If there are countless universes with randomly varying constants, then life-permitting ones are bound to arise somewhere, and of course we find ourselves in one of them - we could hardly find ourselves in a universe that forbids observers. Stated at full strength, that is a coherent and thoughtful move.

But two things keep it from being the refutation it is sometimes taken to be. First, the multiverse is a metaphysical rival to design, not a scientific disproof of it. The mechanisms proposed to generate a multiverse appear to require their own fine-tuning to work - so the problem is arguably relocated rather than removed. And such theories face further puzzles (like the "Boltzmann brain" problem) and have no agreed observational test. Second, and this is the honest point cutting the other way: the design inference itself faces a real technical difficulty, the "measure problem" - specifying a defensible probability over the space of possible universes is genuinely unfinished business, and its best proponents admit as much.

So the fair verdict is that fine-tuning leaves us with serious options - chance (typically via a multiverse), necessity, or design - and no side gets to declare victory. What no one should say is that "scientists agree the universe is designed": the data are widely conceded, but design is a contested philosophical inference. The multiverse does not dissolve the mystery; it is one more serious contender within it.
answers.relatedLabel: Is the Universe Fine-Tuned for Life?

answers.q: Isn't God just wishful thinking?
answers.meta: Wanting God to exist says nothing about whether he does - that cuts both ways. And our deepest longings may be a suggestive pointer, not proof either way.
answers.a:
There is real force in this worry, and it should be granted honestly. Sigmund Freud argued that God is a projection of our wishes - a cosmic father conjured by people who long for protection and meaning. And it is true that human beings often believe what they want to be true. Anyone who has watched comfort masquerade as conviction knows the danger is not imaginary. So the suspicion deserves a fair hearing rather than a quick rebuttal.

But pressed into an argument about whether God actually exists, the wishful-thinking charge quietly commits what philosophers call the genetic fallacy - judging a belief true or false by where it came from rather than by the evidence. Wanting something to be true tells you nothing about whether it is. And crucially, this cuts both ways: a person could just as easily want God not to exist - to be free of accountability or judgment - and that desire would say nothing either. If motives settled truth, atheism would be as vulnerable to the charge as belief. Desire simply is not evidence, in any direction.

There is even a gentler flip side, associated with C. S. Lewis. Our deepest, restless longing - a yearning no earthly thing quite satisfies - might itself be a clue: creatures are generally born with desires that correspond to something real. But this is worth stating modestly. It is at most a suggestive pointer, not a proof, and there is a serious rival account on which such longings are byproducts of evolution. So the honest position is symmetrical: wishful thinking cannot settle the question against God any more than for him. The real work is done not by wishes but by evidence and argument - which is where the conversation should go next.

answers.q: How many Old Testament prophecies did Jesus fulfill?
answers.meta: You'll see numbers like 300 and odds of 1 in 10^17, but that statistic is unsound. The real case is cumulative convergence, not a math proof.
answers.a:
You have probably seen impressive figures - Jesus fulfilled "over 300 prophecies," against odds of one in ten to the seventeenth power. It is worth being candid: that famous statistic, popularized by Peter Stoner, should be retired. It works by assigning made-up probabilities to a handful of prophecies and multiplying them as if they were independent, which they are not. Dressing a real case in false precision does it no favors, and a thoughtful skeptic will see through it at once. So the honest answer is not a number; it is a way of weighing.

A further honesty is needed about what counts as "fulfillment." Some New Testament uses of the Old Testament are predictions - a specific, falsifiable claim like the Messiah's birthplace in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). Others are typology, not prediction: when Matthew applies "Out of Egypt I called my son" (Hosea 11:1) to Jesus, Hosea was plainly speaking of Israel's past, and Matthew is drawing an analogy, not citing a forecast. Lumping the two together inflates the count and misreads the Gospel writers themselves. And several key texts - Isaiah 7:14, Psalm 22, the referent of Isaiah 53 - are genuinely disputed among honest scholars.

What remains, once the weak links are removed, is still substantial - but its shape is cumulative, like a verdict built from many testimonies rather than one decisive proof. The strongest data points are the ones no one could stage: being born in Bethlehem of David's line, and dying by a manner of execution (crucifixion) under Roman power. A first-century Jew could not arrange his own birthplace or engineer a Roman crucifixion, and the texts are demonstrably pre-Christian - the Great Isaiah Scroll dates to roughly 125 BC. So the prophets hand us not a mathematical proof but a portrait, sketched across centuries by many hands, that comes startlingly into focus on one face.
answers.relatedLabel: Messianic Prophecy: Did Jesus Fulfill the Old Testament?

answers.q: Is the virgin birth credible?
answers.meta: A modest, honest case: the virgin birth rests on two independent Gospels and an early, embarrassing tradition - real weight, but held by faith, not proof.
answers.a:
The honest place to begin is with a concession: the virgin birth is not the kind of claim that can be demonstrated like a theorem or investigated like the crucifixion. It is a creedal affirmation resting on the testimony of two Gospels, held by faith with reasons - not a public event open to inspection. Saying that plainly is not a retreat; it is the only honest starting point, and it is where a careful skeptic and a careful believer can actually meet.

Within those limits, though, there are real reasons it carries weight. Matthew and Luke both attest a virginal conception while writing independently - they share almost no wording, yet agree on the core - which is the kind of double, uncoordinated testimony historians take seriously. The tradition is also early: Ignatius of Antioch, around 107 AD, already lists "the virginity of Mary and her giving birth" among the mysteries to be proclaimed, suggesting received tradition rather than late invention. And there is the mark of embarrassment: a pregnancy before marriage invited exactly the charge of illegitimacy that opponents would later throw, hardly a story the church would fabricate to burnish its founder.

Honesty also requires handling Isaiah 7:14 with care, and not overselling it. The Hebrew word there, almah, means a young woman of marriageable age, not specifically a virgin, and its immediate context is a sign to King Ahaz in the eighth century BC. The pre-Christian Greek Septuagint rendered it parthenos, "virgin," and that is what Matthew inherited and quoted. Best treated as typology - a window that illumines the claim - rather than a knockdown prediction. So the case is genuinely modest: real, converging weight from independent and early sources, but not decisive proof. It is affirmed as the Gospels' testimony and the church's doctrine, received by faith with reasons - and it is offered, not imposed.
